nodes.description: Abnormal metaphysis morphology — including flaring (Erlenmeyer-flask appearance) — is a hallmark radiographic finding seen across osteopetrosis subtypes, reflecting accumulation of primary spongiosa in the metaphysis from impaired bone remodeling.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000944 | Abnormal metaphysis morphology | Very frequent (99-80%)
HP:0000944 | Abnormal metaphysis morphology | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Progressive accumulation of unresorbed bone encroaches on the marrow cavity, displacing hematopoietic tissue. This leads to pancytopenia and compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis in the liver and spleen. The hematologic complications are the primary cause of mortality in infantile ARO.
nodes.id: Bone marrow failure due to marrow space obliteration

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, genetic aspects and molecular pathogenesis of osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: increased bone density caused by imbalances in bone formation and remodeling, leading to structural changes in bone tissue, predisposition to fractures, skeletal deformities

evidence.reference_title: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for infantile osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: Infantile osteopetrosis is a lethal disorder resulting from a severe defect in the ability of osteoclasts to resorb bone

nodes.description: Bone pain is a frequent symptom across osteopetrosis subtypes, contributing substantially to morbidity. It reflects microfractures, periosteal stress, and complications of failed bone remodeling.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002653 | Bone pain | Very frequent (99-80%)
HP:0002653 | Bone pain | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: The majority of osteopetrosis cases result from mutations in genes required for acidification of the osteoclast resorption lacuna. TCIRG1 encodes the a3 subunit of the vacuolar proton pump (V-ATPase), and its loss prevents acid secretion into the resorption lacuna. CLCN7 encodes a chloride-proton antiporter that provides the chloride conductance needed to maintain electroneutrality during proton pumping. OSTM1 stabilizes CLCN7. Without adequate acidification, hydroxyapatite dissolution fails and bone matrix cannot be degraded, leading to progressive accumulation of unresorbed primary spongiosa.

evidence.reference_title: Defects in TCIRG1 subunit of the vacuolar proton pump are responsible for a subset of human autosomal recessive osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: we show that TCIRG1, encoding the osteoclast-specific 116-kD subunit of the vacuolar proton pump, is mutated in five of nine patients with a diagnosis of infantile malignant osteopetrosis

evidence.reference_title: Loss of the ClC-7 chloride channel leads to osteopetrosis in mice and man.
evidence.snippet: they fail to resorb bone because they cannot acidify the extracellular resorption lacuna

evidence.reference_title: Osteopetrosis: genetics, treatment and new insights into osteoclast function.
evidence.snippet: mutations in TCIRG1, CLCN7, OSTM1, SNX10 and PLEKHM1 lead to osteoclast-rich ARO (in which osteoclasts are abundant but have severely impaired resorptive function)

nodes.description: Delayed tooth eruption, dental caries, and osteomyelitis of the mandible are common. The dense bone impairs normal tooth eruption and increases susceptibility to jaw infections after dental procedures.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and Management of Osteopetrosis: Consensus Guidelines From the Osteopetrosis Working Group.
evidence.snippet: ongoing monitoring for changes in mineral metabolism and other complications, including cranial nerve impingement, anemia, leukopenia, and dental disease

nodes.description: Compensatory blood cell production in the liver and spleen in response to bone marrow failure. This is a hallmark of the infantile form.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization and management of hypercalcemia following transplantation for osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: markedly abnormal architecture of the marrow space, leading to progressive marrow failure and compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis

nodes.description: Facial nerve palsy results from compression in the narrowed facial canal as dense bone encroaches on cranial nerve VII. This complication occurs in both ADO2 and ARO.

evidence.snippet: HP:0010628 | Facial palsy | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: Osteomyelitis of the mandible secondary to malignant infantile osteopetrosis in an adult.
evidence.snippet: anaemia and thrombocytopaenia. Neurological manifestations can also occur due to

nodes.description: Generalized osteosclerosis, visible throughout the skeleton on plain radiographs, is the defining hallmark of all osteopetrosis subtypes. In ADO2, spine and pelvis predominate; in ARO, uniform dense sclerosis with medullary obliteration is seen.

evidence.snippet: HP:0005789 | Generalized osteosclerosis | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: [Clinical and genetic characteristics of osteopetrosis in children].
evidence.snippet: Clinical manifestations included systemic osteosclerosis (14 cases, 100%)

nodes.description: Conductive and sensorineural hearing loss result from bony overgrowth in the temporal bone, affecting both the ossicles and the cochlear nerve. Hearing impairment occurs in both ARO and ADO2.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization and management of hypercalcemia following transplantation for osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: cranial nerve deficits leading to blindness or deafness

nodes.description: Compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis in the liver and spleen causes hepatosplenomegaly. This is a hallmark of the infantile form and reflects the displacement of hematopoietic tissue from obliterated marrow cavities.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization and management of hypercalcemia following transplantation for osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: hepatosplenomegaly and failure to thrive

nodes.description: Failure of bone resorption reduces calcium mobilization from the skeleton. In TCIRG1-related ARO, impaired gastric acid secretion further reduces dietary calcium absorption. Hypocalcemia may present with neonatal tetany or seizures.

evidence.reference_title: Characterization and management of hypercalcemia following transplantation for osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: hypocalcemic seizures

nodes.description: Generalized osteosclerosis is the hallmark radiographic finding in all forms of osteopetrosis. Dense, sclerotic bone is visible throughout the skeleton, often with loss of corticomedullary differentiation. In ADO2, characteristic endbone (sandwich vertebra) and bone-within-bone patterns are seen.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, genetic aspects and molecular pathogenesis of osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: increased bone density caused by imbalances in bone formation and remodeling, leading to structural changes in bone tissue

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and Management of Osteopetrosis: Consensus Guidelines From the Osteopetrosis Working Group.
evidence.snippet: Osteopetrosis encompasses a group of rare metabolic bone diseases characterized by impaired osteoclast activity or development, resulting in high bone mineral density

nodes.description: Progressive macrocephaly results from calvarial thickening and may be associated with hydrocephalus due to narrowing of the posterior fossa.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000256 | Macrocephaly | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Narrowing of the optic canals by unresorbed bone compresses the optic nerves, leading to progressive visual loss and optic atrophy. This is one of the earliest and most devastating complications in infantile ARO.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and Management of Osteopetrosis: Consensus Guidelines From the Osteopetrosis Working Group.
evidence.snippet: ongoing monitoring for changes in mineral metabolism and other complications, including cranial nerve impingement

nodes.description: Osteomyelitis with jaw predilection — especially mandibular — is a recognized complication of osteopetrosis across subtypes, driven by poor vascularity of the dense sclerotic bone and susceptibility to dental-procedure-triggered infection.

evidence.snippet: HP:0007626 | Mandibular osteomyelitis | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.snippet: Malignant infantile osteopetrosis (MIOP), an autosomal-recessive disorder, is

nodes.description: Obliteration of the marrow cavity by dense bone leads to progressive bone marrow failure with anemia, thrombocytopenia, and leukopenia. This is the principal life-threatening complication in infantile ARO.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and Management of Osteopetrosis: Consensus Guidelines From the Osteopetrosis Working Group.
evidence.snippet: ongoing monitoring for changes in mineral metabolism and other complications, including cranial nerve impingement, anemia, leukopenia, and dental disease

evidence.reference_title: Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for infantile osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: Infantile osteopetrosis is a lethal disorder resulting from a severe defect in the ability of osteoclasts to resorb bone

nodes.description: Despite increased density, osteopetrotic bone is brittle due to disorganized microarchitecture and failure of normal remodeling. Pathologic fractures are common in both ARO and ADO2, particularly affecting the femur.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, genetic aspects and molecular pathogenesis of osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: predisposition to fractures, skeletal deformities

nodes.description: Growth failure in ARO results from chronic illness, impaired endochondral ossification due to persistence of unresorbed cartilage in the growth plate, and metabolic derangements including hypocalcemia.

evidence.reference_title: CLCN7-Related Osteopetrosis.
evidence.snippet: ARO. Onset is at birth. Findings may include: fractures; reduced growth; sclerosis of the skull base
